items.title: Localized Floor Collapse Above Plumbing Pipes
items.body:
I'm looking for some professional advice regarding a floor repair issue in my kitchen. Approximately 10 years ago, a plumber completely redid the plumbing system in my house.

To install the new pipes, he cut through part of the concrete floor, routed the pipes underneath the floor, and then backfilled and repaired the area before reinstalling the flooring.

I've noticed that a small section of the floor (approximately 25 cm x 25 cm / 8–10 inches square, under vinal adessive tile) has started to sag and crumble. This area is directly above the plumbing pipes.

My suspicion is that the concrete or repair material placed over the pipes was too thin, or that the fill underneath has settled over time, creating a void. The floor has not yet completely collapsed, but it appears to be heading in that direction.

One concern is that this area is located in a regular walkway. If the floor eventually gives way completely, someone could step directly onto the pipes, potentially damaging or breaking them.

A few additional details: The finished floor is covered with approximately 2.5 mm vinyl flooring. There appears to be only about 20 mm (3/4 inch) between the top of the pipes and the underside of the floor/concrete above.

I do not currently know whether the pipes are PEX, multilayer, copper, or another material... probably copper.

My current repair idea is:

  1. Remove the vinyl flooring.

  2. Remove all loose and damaged concrete/fill material.

  3. Inspect the pipes and determine the extent of any void.

  4. Fill any voids with a non-shrink structural grout.

  5. Install a 4 mm stainless steel plate extending beyond the damaged area and bearing on sound concrete around the perimeter. I am not sure if this step in needed.. any thoughts?

  6. Apply a thin layer of repair mortar above the plate.

  7. Reinstall the vinyl flooring.

My objective is to distribute foot traffic loads onto the surrounding concrete and protect the pipes from direct loading.

Questions:

Does this seem like a reasonable repair approach?

Would you recommend a different method?

Is the stainless-steel plate unnecessary if the void is properly filled?

Are there any concerns with using non-shrink grout around plumbing pipes?

If only 20 mm of cover exists above the pipes, how would you repair this area professionally?

Is there a better material than a steel plate for this application?

Any advice from plumbers, builders, or concrete repair specialists would be greatly appreciated. Thank you in advance.

items.title: How can I remove a rusted and stripped screw from my dryer cabinet?
items.body: I've tried using a rubber band but had no luck that way... I also tried to cut a notch in the screw head, using a hacksaw, then tried using a slotted (aka "flat") screwdriver to unscrew & remove it. But also no luck that way as well.

items.title: Is it normal that my Bradford White heater has a loud blower?
items.body:
Just installed this new Bradford White heater (48G model, URG2PV50H6N). It heats water great, but my only concern is the extremely noisy blower. It’s not the exhaust air venting from the pipe, but the blower motor itself loud humming and vibration. I had an older Bradford White model that wasn’t nearly as loud in terms of vibration. The installer already tried replacing the blower, but it didn’t help.

The problem is that the vibration is strong enough to shake the house. I can hear it from the third floor, and the unit is installed in the garage.

Is this normal?

items.title: Water under luxury vinyl tile - replace or leave?
items.body:
looking for some experienced opinions before I overreact or underreact.

We had a washing machine leak downstairs Thursday (2/5) around 2:30am. Water spread across about 400 sq ft of floating luxury vinyl tile (Karndean, Rigid Core, Korlok select) installed over a concrete slab.

No wood subfloor. Water volume was enough that it eventually made its way out the front door onto the porch.

We caught it within a couple hours and mopped everything visible. We also ran three fans along the floors. Early on, there were some squishy sounds but those stopped by the next day. I can provide pictures or videos if that helps.

Baseboards are solid wood and don’t appear warped, although there are cracks at the corners, but I’m not sure if those cracks at the joining baseboards were there to begin with.

We started two dehumidifiers from Home Depot Saturday night (~8pm) and ran them most of the time through Sunday night (not perfectly 24/7, but overnight and most of the day). RH with a cheap ThermPro hygrometer with both dehumidifiers running dropped to about 35% in the most affected room.

As of today Monday (2/9) 9am, we turned both dehumidifiers off to do a rebound test. Downstairs is normally cooler than upstairs (two-story living room, single AC unit), so it tends to feel cool even when dry.

Current observations: No more squishy sounds, no musty smell so far, some white line visible in plank seams (seems chalky, not fuzzy)

Planning to check RH again at 12 and 24 hours post shutoff, based on research I’ve done as a complete amateur.

I’m planning to use a General Tools $80 combo pin/pinless moisture meter to compare: baseboards in affected vs unaffected areas slab readings affected vs unaffected areas

My questions:

  1. Have others seen white seam lines like this after a water event that later faded/cleaned off?
  2. In your experience, is mold under floating LVT on concrete likely if moisture readings normalize and stay stable?
  3. If RH stays ≤45–50% after 12–24 hours without dehumidifiers, and baseboards/slab readings match unaffected areas, is it reasonable to leave the LVT in place?

Not trying to cheap out — just trying to avoid unnecessary tear-out if drying was successful. Appreciate any insight from installers, mitigation folks, or homeowners who’ve dealt with something similar.

items.title: Front entrance door and timber frames leak water during heavy rain
items.body: Front timber door (south facing) seeps water through panels into the inside of the door. Adjacent timber frames also get wet. Is it just a case of non water proofed timber?

items.title: Luxaire furnace ignitor shuts off before gas valve opens
items.body:
I have two Luxaire furnaces in the house, same model. They both worked fine until a couple of days ago, when one of them didn't blow warm air anymore. My first thought was the igniter, so I replace it together w/the flame sensor. But comparing the two systems, they act a little different in the timing between the igniter and the gas valve.

On the good one, the igniter turns on, after abt 5 seconds the gas valve opens (I hear a click) and the burner lights up, and after another 2-3 seconds, the igniter turns off. As one would expect.

On the not so good one (the one in which I changed the igniter), the timing between the igniter and the gas valve seems to be a bit off. I see the igniter turning on, after abt 7-8 seconds, it turns off, and only after that the gas valve opens (I hear the click) and the burner ignites as the igniter still has some thermal inertia.

Is there a way to adjust the timing between the igniter and the gas valve from the control board? Or is it something else at play?

Thanks so much for any ideas, Serban.

items.title: Installed new ceiling lighting fixture - now other lights in circuit not working
items.body:
Moved into an old house from 1950s, in Austria. The wiring is fairly old, I assume. In the upstairs master bedroom I replaced the ceiling light fixture today. There are three wires, one blue and two grey - which I’ve never seen before and installed the lighting fixture, after i assumed that one of the grey wires as the Earth wire.

I had to drill two 6mm holes in the ceiling and use plugs for the fixture. Although I didn’t use a wire detector, it didn’t seem like i drilled through any wires. Once i put in a Bulb, the light fixture seems to work fine. But now some of the the lights and outlets that are on the same circuit are no longer working.

I will call an electrician tommorow - but was just wondering if anyone might have an idea what it could be? A short circuit? I left the breaker off in case there is a risk of fire etc., any other safety precautions I could take?

items.title: What is the legal definition of a habitable space/room?
items.body:
I am confused as to what rooms are habitable rooms in regards to permitting. According to ASHI

Habitable rooms are living, sleeping, eating and cooking rooms. Common terms for these rooms include living rooms, family rooms, dens, bedrooms, breakfast rooms, dining rooms and kitchens. ... Bathrooms, laundry rooms, closets, storage rooms, equipment rooms and hallways are not habitable rooms.

An answer, to a different question of mine, suggest that calling a room a study or a kids playroom would make in not be considered a habitable space. To me a study seems a lot like a den. Is there a list of room names that are considered habitable. Is there a list of features that make a room be required to have all the other features of a habitable room?

items.title: Restoring Antique Metal Hardware
items.body:
I would appreciate all help possible in terms of identifying and restoring the material in the photo.

My understanding is: (1) the material is brass-plated steel, and (2) restoration with standard brass polishing products won't work.

The tarnished pole is magnetic - while the tarnished finial is non-magnetic. Therefore, potentially confirming point (1) above regarding the pole as brass-plated steel

Wondered if anyone could help confirm my suspicions and direct me to methods for restoring the handle (if possible).

Would be appreciated.

Thanks

items.title: Will a cordless drill with hammer function work well to drill holes in hard gneiss stone?
items.body:
I have a Milwaukee M18 1/2" Hammer Drill/Driver 3602-20.

I want to anchor a 13-ft x 13-ft shed (up on cinder blocks) to bedrock at an off-grid location in Eastern Ontario, so it doesn't blow over in high winds. The bedrock is bare; there is no soil here. I'll anchor the shed by attaching four chains to the elevated shed floor, and then anchor the chains to the bedrock using concrete anchors. The type of anchors and the drill bits are TBD—dependent on the answer to this question.

Question:

How wide of a hole can the drill/driver make in bedrock, considering its limited power? In other words, how wide of a bit and anchors can I use? It's ok if the drilling is slow.

I know the drill chuck can handle a 1/2" bit, but that's not the same as bedrock drilling capacity.

The Bedrock Geology layer in the Ontario MLAS map seems to suggest the bedrock is gneiss, which I assume is quite hard:

Tectonite Unit - Tectonites, straight gneisses, porphyroclastic gneisses, unsubdivided gneisses in major deformation zones, mylonites, protomylonites

items.title: What can I use to remove caulk from acrylic shower wall seams without damaging the surface shine of new walls?
items.body: I hired someone to install. They used dirty hands to sloppily apply the caulk. It’s all over - clumpy globs of dirty caulk in the seams. And on the shower walls. I need to remove it and do it right. But I don’t want to scratch or remove the shine of the new walls. How do I clean up this mess?

items.title: Will a 65 gallon heat pump water heater remedy occasional shortages with my 50 gallon electric?
items.body:
I'm considering installing one of the Rheem hybrid models at Home Depot (XE80T10H45U1 if we go with 80 gallon, or the 65 gallon version of the same series).

My family of 4 has increasing water consumption as the kids grow up. 50 was just fine until recent years, but once or twice a week, we are having loss of hot water in shower.

We have a small townhouse with the water heater in the basement garage. We are close to San Francisco, CA (a very mild climate).

One extra factor: I have been temporarily running the new dishwasher with cold water (relying on its own heating element), and now I will also add dishwasher as a hot water consumer in the house with these upgrades.

So, should a 65 gallon Rheem hybrid be enough? Add an auxiliary tank? Or not worth it and just go with 80 gallon Rheem hybrid? I am open to broader suggestions.

I'm also wondering whether there is a way to utilize the old tank?

items.title: How to convert an old cast-iron stub into a toilet flange
items.body:
I have a 4" cast-iron pipe sticking about 18" out of the slab in my laundry room, capped with what looks like a threaded cast-iron plug. It ties into the main soil line via a wye, which also carries a wet vent (upstairs bathroom sink, vented to roof).

Rough-in measurements: 13.5" from the side wall, 10.5" from the back wall, both on center.

I don't know what this stub is. Maybe an old soil stack from before a remodel, or some kind of cleanout? The cap is inset rather than flush with or capping the top of the pipe, which makes me wonder if there's a narrower pipe inside it.

I'd like to cut it flush with the floor and install a toilet flange, so I can add a toilet after tiling.

Questions:

  1. What is this pipe likely to be, and does the inset cap tell me anything?
  2. Can I just cut it flush and flange it, or is there more to it?
  3. What's the right way to cut cast iron for this, and what type of flange should I use to transition from cast iron to the toilet?
  4. Is the existing wet vent sufficient for venting a toilet here, or does adding a toilet change the venting requirements?
  5. Anything else worth checking before I commit to cutting it?

items.title: Water leaking from secondary drain line cleanout/vent
items.body:
The home is a split-level, with the kitchen and main level above grade. I discovered a leak from this secondary drain (2") in a below grade-finished bathroom. This is not the main drain line (3"), but I suspect it ties into it beneath the slab. This drain appears to handle all water coming from the kitchen and dishwasher.

The black ABS appears to be a cleanout, however above the P trap to the left, the pipe is completely uncapped. This is where the leak occurred.

Obviously, water should not back up to this level, but is this uncapped pipe with a P trap normal? I'm assuming the P trap is intentional for the open pipe.

I am planning on inspecting and jetting this pipe from the cleanout to resolve the clog but am unsure if it is safe or correct to add a screw cap (if it can be reached).

I am also concerned if this could be indicative of a clog in the main drain line or septic, but I have not inspected the main drain cleanout inside yet.

Here are pictures of the cleanout/standpipe. I did not see this before, but it appears my RO system drains into the top of this pipe from where I can trace the 1/4" tube.

items.title: Concrete pavers from different pallets have slightly different color
items.body:
I ordered 400 blocks of paver stone in Tan color. It was delivered in 2 pallets, but the color of those 2 pallets is slightly different (one is darker, and one is brighter).

Will they change over time under the sun? Is it a defective product?

items.title: Is diamond plate aluminum stronger than sheet aluminum
items.body:
I am looking at building some composite panels for a project (wood on interior, aluminum exterior, foam core) and was wondering if there was any structural reason to use diamond plate aluminum. I have heard from people that diamond plate is stronger (it improves rigidity similar to corrugation), but have not been able to find any data to confirm this, though finding apples to apples comparisons of sheet and diamond plate aluminum with all the variants and tempers has been a challenge.

The closest I have found are these two: https://metalzenith.com/blogs/aluminium-properties/aluminum-4017-composition-properties-temper-guide-applications (which doesn't list yield strength by temper) against the mechanical properties of this https://www.aluminum-hm.com/application/aluminum-tread-plate-4017/ which seem to indicate that if there is an advantage to diamond plate, it isn't really significant, but I should also note that the range for the aluminum sheet is huge.

items.title: Has anyone seen kickback occur on a wet table saw for tile?
items.body:
Most people are familiar with how table saws can kickback when pushing wood through it. I'm talking about a wet table saw with the blade that is housed below the table and protrudes up (as opposed to a chop saw type orientation that other wet table saws are in).

Despite a table wet saw being nearly identical in design, I've never seen any actual kickback evidence with them when cutting a tile. Also likely related, I've been countless people cut tile on a table saw and never seen anyone use a push stick ever. Perhaps the risks of wet saws is far less than a 'standard' table saw.

I'm wondering if kickback is just extremely unlikely to happen, or perhaps due to the diamond blade without teeth, it just basically never happens in reality.

However, upon removing the wallpaper, I noticed cracks in the wall about 5 mm wide and a significant gap between the door frame and the wall. I've attached photos for reference. I'm not very experienced with DIY projects, but I'm willing to give it a try. Any advice on how to fix the cracks in the wall and the gap by the door frame would be greatly appreciated.

WALL CRACK

Door frame Gap

items.title: Pool motor grounding
items.body:
My spa/pool has two 240 volt pumps. They are fed off a subpanel, with separate breakers. The subpanel is fed from the main house panel with 4 wires (hot, hot, neutral, ground). There is no neutral used on the motors.

The pump motors have a ground lug on the frame. The lug on each motor is connected together and then into the subpanel ground via a separate bare copper wire. This connection is outside the motors; i.e. the ground wire does not go from one motor to another in a series, so there is no chance of one motor not being grounded if the other is removed.

The motors also have a ground connection where the power line enters the motor via a conduit. The conduit has 3 wires: (hot, hot, ground). However, this ground wire is not connected inside the subpanel. I have confirmed that the ground lug on the frame is the same as the ground where the power connects to the pump. It has been wired this way for over 20 years but I only noticed it when I was changing the motor bearings.

  1. Should the ground wire in the conduit from the motor to the subpanel box be connected to the ground bar in the subpanel box? Is it okay/better/a problem to have two separate grounds?

  2. Is there any issue with the ground wire to the lugs being bare?

items.title: What type of electrical outlet box should I replace this with?
items.body:
We are doing some reno in our kitchen and I pulled off the backsplash and examined the outlets. They were tightly fitted into old metal boxes to the point where the wire connection points actually touched the box if jostled or installed a mm to the left or right of center. The current box is not attached at all and hangs loose in the opening. The wall is plaster and we will be installing tile onto it.

What type of electrical outlet boxes should I replace them with?

I am assuming a standard old work box will be fine, but as far as I can tell most old work boxes are designed for drywall and not plaster. We will be upgrading these to GFCI if that makes any difference as well.

items.title: Insulating the interior of an old mobile home that already has the original siding + rigid insulation + vinyl siding
items.body:
I own a 1960s mobile home in Michigan and am in the process of a complete renovation. Until now, I've used contractors, and as a bit of background to my question, one talked me into putting up rigid foam insulation over the original metal siding, then putting installing vinyl siding over that. All this was in the interest of cost savings.

Slight aside, the vinyl siding was screwed in (once they realized nails weren't going into anything) and is holding only onto the original metal siding. It seems lousy, but I digress.

I've taken over and want to begin finishing the interior walls. I've removed the insulation, and all that remains is the original vapor barrier on the interior side of the original siding. It's not a perfect seal by any means.

So, from the outside in, we have vinyl siding > rigid foam insulation > original siding > vapor barrier/plastic sheet > framing.

My question: Is insulating the interior the right move? And if so, should I be adding an interior vapor barrier? I had planned to install 1" rigid insulation sealed with spray foam.

items.title: Who should I contact about small but complicated animal intrusion/crumbling?
items.body:
On the outside corner of my house next to the garage door, there is a side door. Around the base of the side door and right at the outside corner, there is "missing" concrete foundation. Like, there's cracks but also actual voids. The brick facade at the front is dipping down ever so slightly. On the inside I tore out some very compromised drywall and the baseboard, behind which was some nuts etc. and the concrete level is lower inside the wall than the garage floor. Recently, I saw a chipmunk squeeze his fat little ass up and under the bottom cedar shingle. Upon investigation, whatever material bridging the gap between the front brick and side structure material (studs I guess?) Is also disappearing away. The drywall issue did seem to be water damage from worn out/insufficient garage door weather stripping. I'm unfamiliar with chipmunks so I'm not sure how much the rest of the damage is caused by them or if they're just taking advantage of the crumbling.

Basically, I'm not sure who to call to fix this. Someone/s who can work with foundation/concrete, siding/exterior, possibly framing if the side door is part of the problem, then I would probably just have it removed (we don't use it).

Even though the issue seems pretty small overall, it's too complicated for me to trust a handyman-type or figure it out myself.

Should I call my homeowners insurance? Do you think they'd cover it or know what to do?

I did replace and add weather stripping when I noticed the drywall dampness.

exterior:

brick facade:

behind brick facade:

interior side door:

items.title: How can I remove paint that didn't bond to glossy trim?
items.body:
I painted a closet including the trim and door frame. I didn't knock the sheen off of the trim materials with sandpaper first. Now it's apparent that the paint isn't going to bond.

I think I'm relegated to removing this paint, sanding, and re-painting. Does anyone have any tips, tools, tricks that would make this paint removal less painful than it seems like it's going to be?

items.title: Mitre Saw 5 degree back bevel not straight but saw is square
items.body:
I am following a guide on YouTube to do a coping joint on molding.

Every time I cut the 5 degree back bevel I start flush and straight in the front but as the cut progresses there is a gap. My mitre saw is square. Its frustrating because coping cuts feel slightly off because I cannot just get a straight 5 degree back bevel cut.

Looks like the 5 is not flush or straight in the front. How can I correct this?

items.title: Tile identification
items.body: Demoing bathroom in a 1924 home. Removing floor tile and under pink tile is a layer of tan cross hatched tile. Don't know if it could be potentially hazardous and wanted to verify it before removal. Edit: Removed it, it was an inch of mud bed with a paper underlayment. Used a spray bottle to wet everything down during removal.

items.title: How can I fix a crack between garage wall and ceiling?
items.body:
This white concrete wall in my garage seems to not be sealed properly against the ceiling. I noticed 2 bubbles like this and when I tore into this one, I see light through it (seems to lead to the roof venting that runs along the edge of the roof).

I assume I could just fill it in and paint over it. Would that be the proper way to repair this? Does this mean that the whole seam on this wall is compromised?

items.title: Same Bosch batteries, but one battery only works in one tool, what can I do?
items.body:
So, I have some BAT612 18V batteries from Bosch.

I should be able to use both batteries with either of my tools (Angle Grinder - GWS18V-45PC, Drill - DDS181A), but one battery works in both tools and the other only works in the drill.

Does anyone know why this is the case?

Or maybe how to fix it?

items.title: Can 15 Amp switches be used on a 20 Amp circuit with lights and receptacles?
items.body: If you run a 20 amp circuit (12/2) with shared lights and receptacles, can you use 15amp switches? It's my understanding that the switch is rated for the load it's carrying (lights), but what about the load of the whole circuit? Lets say you have a room on one 20 amp circuit supplying outlets (15amp rec) and lights (15amp switch), you plug in a vacuum cleaner, have mini fridge running, and a fan plus lights, you pull more than 15 amps on the circuit. The breaker won't flip, but will the light switches rated for 15amp be ok?

items.title: Removing old gas valve
items.body:
I recently had a heat pump installed in my house and removed the old gas line. The meter was removed and the pipe cut at the other end, but the valve where the pipe comes into the house is still there. I phoned my gas supplier, and he confirmed that the pipe was cut properly and I can remove it.

I want to remove it (and already took of the round cover, see pictures), but it seems to be pressed/sealed into the pipe.

The brown ring inside the big pipe looks like translucent brown plastic, it is shiny and one can look a bit through the material. I guess it's some kind of seal.

How do I remove this properly? Just saw it through right next to the pipe?

items.title: Condensate drain line for tankless water heater into vent drain cleanout pipe
items.body:
I’m converting from a tank to tankless condensating gas water heater into an exterior water heater closet. I’ve pulled new GFCI electrical and even added a light and switch.

My question is getting the condensation drain line to drain into a 2” sewer drain pipe in the same closet.

Is here an adapter to run the 1/2” drain line into the cleanout plug? Or is there some type of easy coupling tee available to place inline without removing the white PVC drain vent pipe?

items.title: SubZero Over/Under won't alarm if the freezer door is just slightly open—how can I get an alert?
items.body:
The crude freezer open alert only works if the drawer is opened a few inches—open then almost close it where the gap is a a quarter of an inch, and no alert. You'll feel the cold air on your bare feet and the internal temperature will skyrocket. I complained to SubZero, saying

Your computer knows that the door was open, then perhaps closed, then the temperature started rising. Once it gets like 10 degrees above the set point, it knows the door must be open and could generate an alarm or text message.

Has anyone figured out a way to fix this?

items.title: How much bend is acceptable in 3/4" white PVC for irrigation?
items.body:
We had a break in one of our backyard irrigation pipes, which were installed by the previous owner of the home. I dug up the immediate area and found that the break was at a T-joint. I had an irrigation specialist repair the break. However, after the repair had set, I noticed when testing the repair that the pipe would bend near the T-joint when the sprinklers are turned on and the pipe is under pressure.

I dug out more of the pipe to understand the issue. Here's what the pipes look like in the area around the T-joint where the break occurred.

Pipe A is apparently bent (to the right in the diagram) so that the sprinkler above T2 fits inside a little nook in the foundation of a brick wall. This puts torque on T1, which causes pipe to twist around the T1.

However, what looks even more alarming to me is the significant bend in the run of pipe B, which bends 32" over a distance of 80". This looks to me like it would put significant strain on B and T2.

In summary, there are two issues:

  • Bend in pipe A which causes torque on T1
  • Severe bend in pipe B

How serious are these two issues? Is it enough to warrant repiping some or all of this area?

items.title: Why does the cold water tap initially run hot on summer afternoons before turning cold?
items.body: On hot summer afternoons, the cold-water tap initially delivers hot water. After a few minutes of continuous flow, the water returns to its normal cold temperature. The house has a steel water tank installed on the roof, and the water pipes are inside the walls. What causes this behavior in a residential plumbing system?

items.title: Do I really need to remove grout before caulking bathtub with silicone?
items.body:
I have an older bathtub where there is no caulk/silicone between the tiles (wall) and the tub ... as far as I can tell there is just normal grout. Looks like this:

Or this:

The tub is not used daily but maybe once a week or so. There is no indication of mold or leaks or any issues and I can also see the underside of the tub from the basement. All looks in best order.

However, I do not feel good by this to be only protected by (old) grout and as can be seen from the pictures, there are some hairline cracks and small gaps.

I'd like to run Silicone (GE 100% Supreme or GE Advanced Silicone) along these edges.

Do I really need to remove the old grout first? Or can I directly run the bead?

If I really have to remove: how? I tried with a utility knife but it's really a pain and takes very long to remove small pieces. The grout is just like mortar...

items.title: What do I need to do to prepare this wall for wainscoting and a railing
items.body: We have a crack on our stair wall adjacent to where the banister connects. We are thinking about adding some wainscoting and an additional rail. What steps should I take to prepare/repair the wall before proceeding?

items.title: How to "Shim" a misshapen lintel that will be supported by a new beam
items.body:
Q: Given an iron lintel (rougly 36" across, 3" wide on bottom), that has cupped unevenly from water damage, what do I use to shim it so it's snug with the new supporting beam placed below it?

Details: I have an iron lintel in my basement. It is beginning to lose strength from a few decades of water damage, and has warped a bit. I had a structural engineer report for repair plans, had that plan checked by city, everyone is OK with DIY.

The plan is to put a 6x6 pressure treated wood beam under the lintel, put two 2x6 pressure treated wood boards as posts on each side under the beam (4 total), anchored to the concrete foundation (which forms the door frame). The opening is narrowed by a bit, but the lintel is supported against further structural loss, and pressure treated wood is rot resistant. I'll deal with water intrusion next and separately.

This path was selected because it requires no jacking and allows water / moisture to be handled separately. Everyone called it an "easy fix".

One hitch: What does one use to shim up a 6x6 beam under an iron lintel that is 4" wide (front to back width, the door span is ~36")? I assume pressure treated wood pieces + composite shims where the gap is small? The lintel is mostly level but is irregularly shaped due to uneven expansion, so there is likely to be areas with more or less pressure. In particular, the lintel has some minor "upside down cupping", meaning the middle length of it won't easily shim.

This lintel is clearly bearing some load from the front masonry on my home.

I've considered using some fill like load-bearing mortar, but since it's off the ground and not surrounded by natural containment, that's a mess and hard to get right?

items.title: Radiator for airing cupboard
items.body:
We are replacing our gravity fed hot water cylinder with a larger unvented cylinder. This is going to be fitted adjacent to the boiler, leaving us with more space in the airing cupboard.

Except there will be nothing to keep the airing cupboard warm! I could put a small radiator in there but would rather not use up wall space. So is there such a think as a small, horizontal, floor mounted radiator. I can't find one be googling so I wonder if there is a particular search term that I should be using.

items.title: How to Tile Over a Depressed Floor Drain
items.body:
I am planning to tile my basement floor, but I am stuck on how to handle the existing floor drain. The floor drain is depressed into the basement floor and looks essentially like the image. It's about 2' rim to rim.

I see a few potential paths forward, but I need advice on what is actually feasible or standard practice:

1. Keep the funnel shape: Is it possible to tile a bowl-shaped depression like this using smaller tiles (like mosaics or penny tiles), while transitioning to standard larger tiles for the flat parts of the floor? I've used medium to large format tiles for the rest of my remodel and would like to use those if possible.

2. Fill it in / Level it: Can I use a self-leveling compound or mortar to flatten out the outer edges of the depression, install a drain riser/extension to bring the grate up to the floor height, and make the tiled floor completely flat?

3. Alternative options: Are there other standard ways to handle these older slope basin drains when renovating like a large drain grate?

What are my best options for tiling over or around this type of drain setup?

items.title: Exterior trim board replacement with pressure treated and aluminum flashing - moisture issues or other concerns?
items.body:
The trim boards on one side of an addition are painted, seems to be non-pressure treated wood, and need to be replaced. When I began scraping to repaint, I saw that chunks of wood were falling off at corners, and the wood under the door was disintegrating in a way that made think it wasn't PT. Going forward I don't want to be in a loop of painting maintenance, and I'm looking for a functional and environmentally-friendly option, both for my own ethics and because this is area drains into a nearby food garden.

One contractor I spoke with recommended Azek so that once it's cut and installed it's environmentally neutral for decades until it's landfilled. Another recommended pressure treated wood capped with aluminum flashing: powder-coated aluminum is long-lasting and recyclable, a steel kick-plate can be installed under the door to protect the aluminum, and the wood would be kept out of rain and not need to be painted.

My question is, is there a reason not to go with PT wood and aluminum flashing? Is there a risk of moisture getting trapped behind caulked flashing? The existing painted wood was caulked but that has long decayed, so moisture can get in there (when it's stormy or snowy enough for moisture to build up under the eaves) but it also gets south-facing sun and can dry out.

For reference, the other trim boards around this room were non-painted wood (seemed to be pressure treated) when we got the place a few years ago, and we had that capped with white aluminum flashing and caulk too. Here's a before and after of what that looked like. Note when we got here the white-painted board I'm replacing was actually jutting out a few inches, maybe having been attached to a wooden deck that wrapped around the room at one point.

Exposed wood trim board when we first moved:

And aluminum trim added over the unpainted wood trim:

items.title: What size ball valve (or adapter) do I actually need?
items.body:
My stove was installed with a flexible connector that is labeled 5/8" OD. This connector connects directly to the gas supply from the wall, so I (maybe incorrectly) assume that piece would also be 5/8". I can't find a 5/8" male to female ball valve anywhere, but I have a 1/2" to 1/2". Do I actually need a different size of valve that exists? Or, exactly what kind of adapter would I need on each side of the 1/2" valve I have? I tried buying adapters that called themselves 1/2" to 5/8" for each side of the valve, but they mostly don't seem to fit anything* and I'm not even completely convinced they are the sizes they say they are.

The valve I have is here

*Oddly, one of them seems to screw into both ends of the valve I have, but that's not very helpful.

items.title: How do I fix a metal storm door that is too low on its hinges?
items.body: We have an aluminum glass door outside of our front door that won't close or open without serious force. It looks like it needs to be lifted on the hinges, as pictured. There is a gap at the top of door and it hits on the bottom of door frame. New homeowner, how can I fix this?

items.title: How can I identify the manufacturer and model/date of this electrical panel?
items.body:
My new insurer is asking me to show the make and model of my electrical panels to determine if I have any recalled Murray panels. My inside subpanel is a Murray but not one of the model/dates in the recall. My outside panel however appears like it might be old, and it's missing a manufacturer label, which it seems I'm going to need an electrician to inspect.

While I'm waiting, do any electricians recognize this panel and if it might be a Murray?

Does the fact that it has no label mean it will have to be replaced?

items.title: Does every deck stair with stringers longer than 6 feet need mid-span support?
items.body:
I have read that cut stair stringers longer than 6 feet in length require mid-span support. Solid stringers seem to be able to get to about 13 feet without support. So how do you combine these two ideas when you are building 4 foot wide stairs and the two outside stringers are solid and the two inside stringers are cut?

Can that span the entire length unsupported, or does the presence of the two cut stringers bring you back to a maximum of 6 feet unsupported? In my case, the total rise will be about 8 feet or so, for stringers somewhere around the 13 feet mark.

Or is this simply a better-safe-than-sorry thing and you just support it always?

items.title: How do I stop my GE dishwasher GDF620HMJ5ES from beeping when not in use?
items.body: My GE dishwasher started beeping randomly when not in use a few months ago. It still washes dishes fine, but about every 10-30 minutes, it gives an incremental beep.Beep.BEEP.

items.title: How to attach/level/shim deck beams resting directly on concrete (no posts)
items.body:
Our house has a small and low deck (top of deck is ~18" above the ground), which I'm guessing is 20+ years old. The decking itself was rotting in places, so I removed it. My intent was simply to replace the decking and a couple of joists (which aside from a few are in surprisingly decent shape). However, I have decided to go ahead and replace all of the joists.

Presently, joists are attached to the house on one side and supported by 4x6 beams on the other side of the deck (see photo). Joists are cantilevered a small distance on that side of the deck.

My primary question concerns the beams. The existing beams are resting unattached on poured concrete footings or piers - there are no posts between concrete and beams, and it really isn't possible to add posts because deck would then be too high (there is a house door out onto the deck). There are wooden shims between concrete and beam.

For a few reasons I am thinking I am going to float the deck on the house side instead of keep its present attachment to the house. If I do that, I could rest a beam on concrete blocks more easily than pouring concrete footings (not ideal, I know, but I think it would suffice). That beam would support the joists on the house side.

If I add a beam on the house side and also replace those pictured beams that meet at an obtuse angle, how do I go about securing them, so they don't move laterally, and making them level? (Maybe the deck's weight will keep them from moving and maybe shimming as has been currently working for 20+ years is fine, but it seems a bit hacky.)

items.title: How do I remove the temperature indicator on this old thermostatic cartridge?
items.body: There is this black temperature ring that has broken, and apparently they don't make this part anymore. I'm wondering if there is a way to take the temperature ring off the cartridge so that I can glue it back together. I couldn't see any obvious screws visible except what looks like a place where a giant flathead would fit, but nothing that I have is actually able to turn it. Is there a specialized tool? Am I going in the right direction?

items.title: Open Door shower with glass panels causes water to puddle and then leak out the opening
items.body:
We recently had our tub and shower replaced with two shower heads. Entrance is in the middle with shower heads on each end. There is not a door, only two glass panes on each side. One of the sides puddles up on the curb and then eventually leaks onto the bathroom floor through the entrance. I don't think the contractor understands the problem or knows how to fix.

The shower head angles are in a good place. The water builds up during the shower and then starts to flow out.

We do not want plastic guards on the corners at the bottom. How can we fix this?

items.title: Venting to above bathroom sink and proper angles
items.body:
Background - I have a Saniflo Sanicompact toilet in my basement which is self contained and doesn't need to be vented. There is a bathroom sink that drains into it. Saniflow folks said that the sink doesn't need to be vented but I could put an AAV on it to stop the gurgling that would happen in the toilet when the sink would run. The sink trap would also get sucked dry and gas would leak out. The AAV didn't help the smell besides move it from the sink to the AAV. I knew I wanted to properly vent it to the bathroom sink above once we started our remodel there, which is where I am at now.

I want to make sure my venting is correct since I am tired of not having this done right in the first place. I currently just have the AAV unscrewed and screwed the temporary vertical PVC in place. Basically I would run PVC up and over with 90 degree fittings. The horizontal would have a slope. Then up through the floor to the connect to the sink vent above using a tee.

  1. Does this all look good?
  2. Looking at the second pic, the fitting where the vertical PVC is now isn't completely straight. It has an angle out from the wall to accommodate the old AAV sitting against the wall. The vertical pipe does need to bump out to go around the foam wrapped copper pipes that are strapped to the wall above. My understanding is the vertical pipes in a vent need to be actually vertical. Should I just do a 45 out from the wall and back in to a vertical pipe up to the first 90? Or even back to back 90s? 3. Do I need to cut out that fitting and position a new one to be vertical before bumping out the pipe to up?

Thanks for the help here.

Current sink vent

items.title: Gas Dryer suddenly trips any GFCI Outlet immediately upon being plugged in, but works fine in non GFCI
items.body:
I have a Maytag Centennial gas clothes dryer in my laundry room. It is connected to a non-gfci outlet which is downstream of another outlet with built in gfci. It has been connected like this and working fine since I bought the house two months ago, and presumably has always been connected to this outlet for the 15 years that house has existed.

Tonight we discovered the GFCI outlet tripped and wouldn't stay reset and upon further investigation determined that without the dryer plugged in, everything worked ok but as soon as the dryer was plugged in (even though not turned on), the gfci immediately tripped. If I connect the dryer (via an extension cord), to a GFCI outlet in my kitchen (different circuit), it also trips that one, but if I connect it to a non-GFCI outlet it doesn't trip the breaker and runs fine.

My research on line has brought up posts saying the gas dryers should not be plugged into GFCI outlets at all as they may spuriously trip them, but this dryer has been working in this configuration for 15 years, so I'm guessing something has recently gone wrong with it in the past two days since we used it last.

My question is whether I should be looking into getting a non-gfci outlet installed for the dryer or if I should be looking to get the dryer repaired, and if so is there anything I should look for in the dryer myself rather than call service.

items.title: Options with existing basement bathroom rough in
items.body:
I'm trying to interpret this existing basement rough-in enough to understand my options and not back into too many corners. Most important thing: not looking to actually develop this bathroom right away (it's fine being an unfinished storage closet for many years as far as I'm concerned), but I'd like to develop the rest of the basement soon. I'd like to have as much space as possible for other things while leaving the option of a small 3-piece (preferably with tub) bathroom down the road.

Questions:

  1. Looking at the existing rough in, I can't see how I wouldn't have to modify it or somehow work around it to meet code (Alberta, Canada). My really naive understanding is that the toilet drain is way too close to the shower/tub drain rough in. Is this correct? Or am I looking at it wrong. Options as I understand them would somehow be raising the floor so plumbing could be routed underneath (while making sure there's still enough height to meet code). Or jackhammering and moving the toilet drain. It doesn't bother me too much (again, don't feel an overwhelming need to develop this)... just really puzzled as most other things in this house seem really well thought out (was built in the early 90s).
  2. What are the minimum dimensions I'd want to leave here to not have too much of a headache developing it down the road, assuming I'd put a tub or shower lengthwise along the "wall" labelled in the diagram (ideally with a bit of "oops" breathing room).
  3. Anything else important I should be considering when putting up walls here for the current storage area?
  4. Any other options I'm not considering that strike those of you who've worked with these before?

items.title: Harbor breeze dimmable bulb replacements
items.body: I have a Harbor Breeze Lynstead ceiling fan model #00872. I use a Harbor Breeze dimmable remote model RCE-103LW. What light bulb(s) are compatible?

items.title: Best paint remover for hardwood floors?
items.body:
Whats the best way to remove paint off of hardwood floors?

We were painting my daughters room and didn't notice she accidentally got paint on her shoe till after it was too late.

We've tried paint thinner, goo gone, and scraping it with a razor, but we don't want to stain or ruin the floor. Any ideas?

items.title: How to remove this type of stain from marble/stone floor?
items.body:
In reference to the photo, these are stains I spotted when looking directly under light right beside my bath mat. Its also very close to my toilet. I'm not sure if there stains due to heavy water or possible urine stains. The floors weren't sealed.

When the lights aren't directly over, I can also see a hazy/foggy effect over the marble floor.

I tried just some standard marble/granite cleaner and scrubbed the floor in a circular motion with a sponge (soft side) but didn't do anything. Any suggestions on how I should go about removing this particular type of stain?

items.title: Shutting off water inside the house
items.body:
Due to a family emergency I might have to go on an extended leave for over a month. I want to ensure there is no sudden flooding inside the house while away without the need to turn off the water main, as I will request a friend to help with watering my plants in the backyard while I am away.

I believe turning off all stop valves inside the house would be enough?

And for the gas water heater tank, I plan to shut off the cold water inlet, but which setting should I switch it to?

items.title: How to insulate and wall off basement partition for best long-term air quality outcomes?
items.body:
Background

Project description: U.S. heating zone 5, in a 1960s ranch-style house with basement matching original home footprint, we're partitioning the empty half of the basement (no mechanicals, laundry, etc.) to use remaining space for office, hangout, couches and kids space. Below is a couple photos of the space with framing done. In the half that's being finished, we left a 3ft utility corridor for 1 of the 2 sump pumps, kitchen plumbing, and electrical to the kitchen and garage. Framed out space includes stairs up to center of house, a new door to new utility corridor, and new door to the rest of the unfinished half (which leads to walk-out door to yard).

Water context: The half being finished is below grade and around that the surface is all concrete slabs (front porch, back porch, garage slab and driveway). Unfinished half of basement is surrounded by yard graded away from house and has a walk-out door slightly below grade, plus a few windows at or partially-below ground-level.

Basement perimeter has an interior drain, installed in August 2023 (along with 2 updated sump pits and pumps) due to some water seepage at edge of slab and walls and mainly because of flooding from outdoor stairwell drain failures. That was a high quality install, creating a drainage channel under the thick concrete floor slab, with weep holes to drain the cinderblocks, and with waffleboard to catch any condensation dripping down walls. Since that system was installed, no water intrusion has been observed, outdoor stairwell is all good.

This space has 1 dehumidifier running on and off throughout the day to maintain 45-50% RH, and if needed we'll add a 2nd dehumidifier for use while the finished space is occupied. There is no plumbing above the space to be finished (and unlikely to ever be), so water problems would likely come from condensation, bulk water on floor (flooding or basement appliance breakdown), or extreme spill/roof leak from above.

Photos

Here's a look at the half being finished, with a view into the walkway for that utility corridor:

Here's a view from in the half being finished, showing interior french drain and showing doorway to utility corridor:

And in case it's helpful context, here's a peak underground from the interior drain install (ignore the hose going out the wall):

Framing so far: I thought sill plates needed to be pressure treated but contractor insisted they did not. They had great ratings and long-time experience in the area, and I heard mixed opinions about PT sill plate, so I trusted them on it. They said the room is not an exterior space needing PT wood, since it is not in contact with exterior walls and the slab underneath is thick and drained (so it won't wick up moisture). They framed the wall 1" or more away from exterior cinderblock walls and waffleboard.

Finishing it

My question is about insulation and drywall to minimize risk of moisture / mold issues in this area. The priority is air quality, as thermal inertia is not a big issue here: it is not a bedroom, it is decently comfortable unfinished (temperature ranges 55F to 70F over last two years) and it has heating options of either wood or electric. There's an existing ceiling with old fiberglass bats, foil side down which seems incorrect but no problems yet. That old ceiling insulation extends to rim joists in a patchwork way, and this partition will block access to those rim joist cavities.

What is the appropriate way to finish this wall assembly, to minimize risks of moisture damage anywhere, mold or other air quality issues? For example, fiberglass vs Rockwool vs ??? and should it have some kind of backing or not? Vapor barrier somewhere in assembly (like between drywall and studs) or not? Drywall should be anything in particular, or installed in a particular way? Any other important considerations before we close it up?

I'm not asking for opinions based on personal preference, I'm asking to understand which construction approach is most appropriate given building sciences of air flow, vapor flow, below grade construction, material safety for indoor air quality, etc. I've ready lots about vapor barriers and convection loops but am still unsure the way to go. These various details seem like interrelated mini-questions about the overall wall assembly, so I'm including it as 1 question with all this context.

items.title: Is this new Hardie/Window installation going to leak?
items.body:
LINK TO PHOTOS

Hardie siding was installed at my house and it doesn't look quite right to my (untrained) eye. I'm concerned that this install cause issues, whether by (a) immediate water issues/leaks or (b) causing the Hardie to prematurely fail.

Essentially, the Hardie trim around the windows was installed on top of Hardie siding planks. Everything is caulked where the windows meet the trim, but not where the trim meets the siding planks.

The contractor is saying it's completely fine and won't cause water leak issues.

Questions:

  • Is it OK for the trim to overlay the siding at windows?
  • Is the window flashing + trim installed properly?
  • Do I need to caulk where the Hardie trim overlays the siding around the windows vertically?
  • Do I need to caulk where the Hardie trim overlays siding horizontally on the bottom or on top? (My understanding is NOT on top/by the flashing.)
  • Do I need to caulk where the Hardie trim overlays the siding on the outside corners?
  • Do you see anything else iffy?

If this is all wrong, is there a way to fix without having to remove the trim, cut the existing siding, and replace the trim so the siding butts up against it?

Any nuance you have have re: "this is improper per Hardie recommendations, but unlikely to cause issues" vs. "this is improper and definitely will cause issues" would be appreciated.

Thanks for your help and input.

items.title: How to refix screws that pulled away from melamine/chipboard
items.body:
My kitchen cabinets are Ikea-style (actually Häfele) melamine/chipboard, 16mm thick. I noticed one of the hinges was loose, so I unscrewed it and it turned out that the previous owner had already 'repaired' it, with oversized rawlplugs. The top rawlplug came away when I removed the screw; the bottom one is still there:

Here is the hinge:

Can this be fixed, using the basic tools that I possess (power drill + standard hand tools)?

One thought I had was to drill all the way through and use bolts. What's the best way to make this look good from the outside?

items.title: Can I use RedGard on my shower drain?
items.body:
One day my wife was showering and some water starting coming out of a light fixture underneath the shower. I opened it up and didn't see any leaking pipes, and determined it was around the gasket interface in the actual drain itself.

To address this, I have been using a layer of clear waterproof silicone on top of the black gasket part, and it has helped but after time the silicone degrades and comes off. Can I instead apply one or two coats of RedGard waterproof membrane on the gasket and surrounding areas? Does anyone see an issue with this? Or another recommendation?
